examples.english: I stumbled upon a jar of gold.

senses.gloss: track, furrow, trail, what is there after one draws (جَرَّ (jarra))

etymology.text:
Compare Hebrew גֵּרָה (gerá, “cud”).

See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

body.text: an earthen vessel; a vessel made of potters' clay:
, (T, S, Msb, K,) [or this last is rather a coll. gen. n., signifying
; or, accord. to some, this is a dial. var. of

made in such a vessel is forbidden in a trad.: (Mgh, TA:) but accord. to IAth, the trad. means a vessel of this kind glazed within, because the beverage acquires strength, and ferments, more quickly in a glazed earthen vessel. (TA.)       -A2-  See also
:       -A3-  and see what here next follows.
a piece of wood about a cubit long,
having a snare at the head,
and a cord at the middle,

(S, K:) when the gazelle is caught in it, he strives with it awhile, and struggles in it, and labours at it, to escape; and when it has overcome him, and he is wearied by it, he becomes still, and remains in it; and this is what is termed [in a prov. mentioned below] his becoming at peace with it: (S, * TA:) or it is
stick, tied to a snare, which is hidden in the earth, for catching the gazelle; having cords of sinew;

when his fore leg enters the snare, the cords of sinew become tied in knots upon that leg; and when he leaps to escape, and stretches out his fore leg, he strikes with that staff, or stick, his other fore leg and his hind leg, and breaks them. (AHeyth, TA.)
and then became at peace with it

[see above] is a prov. applied to him who opposes the counsel, or opinion, of a people, and then is obliged to agree: (S, * TA:) or to him who falls into a case, and struggles in it, and then becomes still. (TA.) And it is said in another prov.,
He is like him who searches in the earth for the
, in a saying of Ibn-Lisán-el-Hummarah, referring to sheep, [app. meaning
When they escape from their two states of danger,
he means their place of pasture (

) in a severe season [when they are liable to perish], and when they are scattered, or dispersed, by night, and [liable to be] attacked, or destroyed, by the beasts of prey: so says ISk: Az says that he calls their
two snares, into which they might fall, and perish. (TA.)
manner, of dragging, drawing, pulling, tugging, straining,
of the camel, and of a clovenhoofed animal: this is the primary signification: by extension of its meaning, it has the signification next following. (Msb.) ―         -b2-  The
: (K:) it is said to belong to the same predicament as

body.text_ar: لَا اَفْعَلُ ذٰلِكَ مَا ا@خْتَلَفَتِ الدِّرَّةُ وَالجِرَّةُ

body.text: I will not do that as long as the flow of milk and the cud go
The flow of milk was procured by the cud
]: alluding to the beasts' becoming full of food, and then lying down and not ceasing to ruminate until the time of milking. (IAar, TA.) And
he will not conceal a secret:
he does not speak when affected with rancour,
:) [or the last has the contr. signification: for]
he will not be silent respecting that which is in his bosom, but will speak of it.
mouthful with which the camel diverts and occupies himself until the time when his fodder is brought to him.
